target.title: feat(memory): add Memory Audit review queue
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

- Adds Memory Audit as a human-reviewed memory quality pass for cleanup, correction, and promotion from daily/session evidence into durable targets.
- Exposes audit collection/staging tools, Gateway doctor methods, and a dashboard Audit tab with review and settings flows.
- Adds managed daily/weekly audit cron reconciliation while keeping Memory Audit separate from Dreaming promotion.

Why does this matter now?

- Dreaming is useful as an automated promotion loop, but in practice it can promote low-value or stale fragments into durable memory, and it does not put those writes through a human review queue.
- Memory Audit is the safer alternative for memory quality work: it uses an LLM to inspect writable memory targets plus daily memory and session-log evidence, stage add/edit/delete/move recommendations, and leave the final apply/reject decision to a human.
- The current PR targets `AGENTS.md`, `MEMORY.md`, `USER.md`, `TOOLS.md`, and `shared-memory.md`, while treating `memory/*.md` daily files and session logs as read-only evidence for promotion.

What is the intended outcome?

- Operators can enable Memory Audit, review staged recommendations, apply/reject items, and let managed cron jobs collect/stage future suggestions from daily memories and session logs into the right writable target.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- Replacing or removing Dreaming.
- Mutating raw session logs or historical daily memory files during apply.
- Adding `IDENTITY.md` and `SOUL.md` as a targets in this PR.
- Live production rollout proof beyond local Gateway/config/test validation.

What does success look like?

- Memory Audit settings and review UI work through the dashboard.
- The owning memory plugin is enabled when Memory Audit is enabled.
- Managed audit cron jobs reconcile reliably after Gateway restart/startup.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Config/restart behavior for `plugins.entries.memory-core.config.memoryAudit`.
- Gateway doctor method scope and handler registration.
- Memory Audit apply/reject behavior and file-surface safety.
- Dashboard settings/review UX and cron reconciliation behavior.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

- None.

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

- None.

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

- No explicit linked issue in this PR body.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Memory Audit adds a human-reviewed memory cleanup queue, audits daily memory and session-log evidence, stages promotion into `AGENTS.md`/`MEMORY.md`/`USER.md`/`TOOLS.md`/shared memory, and fixes enablement so enabling audit also enables the owning memory plugin and requests a safe Gateway restart for schedule reconciliation.
- Real environment tested: local source checkout in the `feature/memory-audit` worktree after rebasing on latest `upstream/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
  - `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.ts src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.ts ui/src/i18n/locales/*.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
  - `node scripts/format-docs.mjs docs/concepts/memory-audit.md`
  - `git diff --check`
  - Scope check: `git diff --name-only upstream/main...HEAD | rg '^(src/tasks/|extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager)' || true`
- Evidence after fix: all targeted passing commands above passed locally; the scope check returned no task-flow or QMD mcporter files.
- Observed result after fix: Memory Audit tests cover `AGENTS.md`, daily memory, session-log evidence, read-only evidence safety, and promotion into writable targets; UI tests cover new kind labels; cron tests cover the updated audit prompt and startup retry reconciliation; gateway reload tests preserve the Memory Audit restart-required contract.
- What was not tested: full end-to-end dashboard interaction in a browser and a live Gateway restart that creates persistent audit cron jobs from a real operator click. `src/gateway/server-methods/doctor.test.ts` was attempted but could not import in this worktree because `src/gateway/protocol` is absent.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: local tests and source-level validation only; no screenshots or remote/live artifact attached.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): local diagnosis showed `memoryAudit.enabled=true` while `plugins.entries.memory-core.enabled=false`, with no Memory Audit cron jobs present.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-memory.config.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory-audit-cron.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run ui/src/ui/controllers/memory-audit.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/memory-audit.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway.config.ts src/gateway/config-reload.test.ts`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:sync`
- `pnpm ui:i18n:check`
- `git diff --check`

What regression coverage was added or updated?

- Memory Audit review queue, apply/reject behavior, `AGENTS.md` target handling, daily memory/session-log evidence collection, read-only source safety, Gateway doctor payload typing, dashboard controller/view coverage, managed cron reconciliation, i18n output, and config reload planning.

What failed before this fix, if known?

- Enabling Memory Audit could leave `memory-core` disabled, so the dashboard looked enabled while plugin hooks/tools and managed cron jobs were unavailable.
- Audit cron reconciliation only ran at `gateway_start` and could miss cron service availability during startup ordering.

If no test was added, why not?

- Tests were added and updated.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

Yes.

What is the highest-risk area?

- New Gateway doctor methods/tools and managed cron scheduling that can inspect durable memory surfaces and stage recommendations.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Suggestions are staged for human review rather than applied automatically.
- Gateway method scopes and handler registration are covered by tests.
- Memory Audit config changes retain the restart-required contract and managed cron reconciliation has focused coverage.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

- Draft PR for maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

- CI and any maintainer-requested live/dashboard proof.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

- None yet.

<!-- agent-transcript:start -->
## Agent Transcript

<details>
<summary>Redacted codex session transcript</summary>

````text
source: [LOCAL_SESSION]
redaction: local paths, emails, phone-shaped strings, token-shaped strings, auth headers, auth query params
omitted: raw tool outputs, system/developer prompts, local paths, secrets, browser/session/auth details
stats: {"agent":"codex","entries":17,"user":8,"assistant":8,"toolCalls":398,"toolOutputsDropped":397,"web":0,"redactions":0,"omittedUnsafe":0,"rawEntries":65}

[user]
I have a local branch in its worktree: feature/memory-audit, and it is combined into my current branch feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527
  I have this enabled in my openclaw dashboard, however, I think when I enabled it, it says it will restart gateway but it actually did not restart the gateway (and is restarting
  necesary?); Also, there are no cron jobs created related to memory audit as the feature intended, why?

[assistant]
I’ll first ground this in the actual branch/worktree state and the memory-audit implementation, then I’ll separate “current behavior” from “intended behavior.” I’m using the OpenClaw debugging workflow for this investigation.

The current checkout is on `feature/combined-flow-qmd-memory-audit-20260527`, ahead 13 and behind upstream/main by 270; the separate `feature/memory-audit` worktree is `/home/syu/openclaw-memory-audit`. I’m narrowing the search now because the first broad scan hit generated/bundled content.

The branch adds Memory Audit in 61 files, including the dashboard, `memory-core`, gateway config reload, and docs. The key clue so far: Memory Audit config is explicitly marked as a restart-required config prefix in the gateway reload planner, so I’m checking whether the dashboard’s “restart scheduled” message actually maps to a real service restart.

Two independent restart paths exist: `config.patch` can directly schedule SIGUSR1 only for `gateway.reload.mode=hot` or `off`, while the normal/hybrid path relies on the running gateway’s config watcher to detect the persisted config write and then request restart. I’m now checking why the cron reconciliation is gated on startup rather than on a hot plugin reload.

The live read-only checks show Memory Audit config is enabled with daily and weekly schedules, but `cron list` reports a warning that `plugins.entries.memory-core` is disabled while config is present, and there are 0 Memory Audit jobs among 19 cron jobs. That makes plugin activation the leading cause rather than cron itself.

There is also a startup race: Memory Audit only reconciles cron in a `gateway_start` hook, and that hook can run before the cron service is exposed. Dreaming already has retry/runtime reconciliation for this exact class of problem; Memory Audit currently does not.
````

target.title: fix: rebuild legacy flow_runs tables
target.body:
## Summary

This PR now keeps the TaskFlow SQLite migration fix already present on current `main` and adds focused regression coverage for upgraded `flow_runs` table shapes.

The tests prove that legacy tables retaining `owner_session_key` are rebuilt to the canonical `owner_key` schema, remain writable for new managed flows after migration, and handle hybrid upgraded rows where `owner_key` exists but is blank.

## What changed

- extend the legacy `owner_session_key` migration test to verify managed-flow writes still work after rebuild
- assert rebuilt `owner_key` is `NOT NULL` and `owner_session_key` is removed
- add coverage for hybrid tables with blank `owner_key` values falling back to `owner_session_key`

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/tasks/task-flow-registry.store.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Upgraded SQLite stores with legacy or hybrid `flow_runs` schemas must migrate to the canonical `owner_key` schema and accept managed flow writes.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, real TaskFlow registry/store modules, and Node SQLite.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a one-off `node --import tsx --input-type=module` script that created a hybrid legacy `flow_runs` table in a temporary OpenClaw state directory, loaded the real TaskFlow registry, restored the legacy row, created a new managed flow, and inspected the rebuilt SQLite schema.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output from that one-off proof script:

```json
{
  "restored": {
    "flowId": "hybrid-flow",
    "ownerKey": "agent:proof:legacy-hybrid",
    "syncMode": "managed",
    "controllerId": "core/legacy-restored",
    "revision": 0,
    "status": "queued"
  },
  "created": {
    "flowId": "0731cbae-eb13-4819-b7e5-6bd915b1d77a",
    "ownerKey": "agent:proof:fresh",
    "syncMode": "managed",
    "controllerId": "proof/migrated-flow",
    "status": "queued"
  },
  "schema": {
    "hasOwnerSessionKey": false,
    "ownerKeyNotNull": 1
  },
  "rows": [
    {
      "flow_id": "hybrid-flow",
      "owner_key": "agent:proof:legacy-hybrid",
      "sync_mode": "managed",
      "controller_id": "core/legacy-restored",
      "revision": 0,
      "status": "queued"
    },
    {
      "flow_id": "0731cbae-eb13-4819-b7e5-6bd915b1d77a",
      "owner_key": "agent:proof:fresh",
      "sync_mode": "managed",
      "controller_id": "proof/migrated-flow",
      "revision": 0,
      "status": "queued"
    }
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: The hybrid legacy row restored with `owner_key` from `owner_session_key`, the rebuilt table no longer had `owner_session_key`, `owner_key` was `NOT NULL`, and a new managed flow inserted successfully after migration.

What was not tested: A full webhook HTTP round trip was not run in this PR; this proof exercises the real persistence path used by managed flow creation.

target.title: fix(plugins): persist resolved npm install specs
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve normal npm update selectors in plugin and hook install records.
- Keep npm resolution output as provenance metadata (`resolvedName`, `resolvedSpec`, `integrity`) instead of turning ordinary selectors into active exact pins.
- Keep exact active specs only for explicit exact selectors / explicit pin behavior.

## Context
Follow-up to #78588 and the review finding that the previous approach pinned ordinary npm update selectors to old exact versions.

The corrected behavior keeps `record.spec` as the requested/update selector. Resolution metadata remains available for provenance, lock/trust checks, and diagnostics without changing the selector that future updates use.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Normal npm update selectors must remain in `record.spec`; successful npm resolution must only populate provenance metadata unless the user explicitly pins / uses an exact selector.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw PR worktree on Linux, commit `975a9e45a9701fee14fb11d6e4fedbf93494b796`, using the patched TypeScript source via `tsx`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
```sh
npm exec tsx tmp/pr81100-real-proof.mjs
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the patched worktree:
```json
{
  "unpinned": {
    "recordSpec": "@openclaw/plugin-alpha@latest"
  },
  "pinned": {
    "recordSpec": "@openclaw/plugin-alpha@1.2.3",
    "pinNotice": "Pinned npm install record to @openclaw/plugin-alpha@1.2.3."
  },
  "record": {
    "source": "npm",
    "spec": "@openclaw/plugin-alpha@latest",
    "installPath": "/tmp/openclaw/plugin-alpha",
    "version": "1.2.3",
    "resolvedName": "@openclaw/plugin-alpha",
    "resolvedVersion": "1.2.3",
    "resolvedSpec": "@openclaw/plugin-alpha@1.2.3",
    "integrity": "sha512-demo"
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The unpinned selector stays active as `record.spec = @openclaw/plugin-alpha@latest`, while `resolvedSpec = @openclaw/plugin-alpha@1.2.3` and `integrity = sha512-demo` remain provenance metadata. The explicit pin path still records the exact resolved spec.

**What was not tested:** No additional gaps.

Supplemental validation:
- `npm test -- src/cli/npm-resolution.test.ts src/cli/plugins-cli.install.test.ts src/plugins/update.test.ts src/hooks/update.test.ts src/security/audit-plugins-trust.test.ts src/commands/doctor/shared/missing-configured-plugin-install.test.ts src/commands/onboarding-plugin-install.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/commands-plugins.install.test.ts src/commands/channel-setup/plugin-install.test.ts`
- `npm run tsgo:test:src`
- `npm run tsgo:core`
- `git diff --check`

## Notes
- The audit remains strict for active exact specs/integrity; provenance-only `resolvedSpec` is not treated as the update selector.

target.title: Memory/QMD: isolate mcporter sidecars per agent
target.body:
## Summary

- Isolate QMD mcporter daemon/config state per agent by writing an agent-scoped mcporter config and passing it to mcporter calls.
- Preserve the configured mcporter server definition instead of replacing custom/remote server config with a hard-coded local `qmd mcp` entry.
- Keep QMD model/env settings flowing only through the generated stdio server entry, while stripping QMD-scoped XDG env from mcporter's own process environment.
- Tolerate mcporter daemon log lines before JSON responses so warm daemon calls do not break memory search parsing.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.ts extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/memory/qmd-manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm run lint:extensions:bundled`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** QMD memory search through mcporter must keep each agent on its own mcporter config/daemon state while preserving the configured `qmd` mcporter server and avoiding QMD-scoped XDG env leakage into mcporter's own process env.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with Node `v24.15.0`, `mcporter 0.10.2`, `qmd 2.1.0`, configured agents `hex` and `trump`, and the configured mcporter `qmd` stdio server. Home paths are redacted as `~`; private snippets are omitted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```text
node --version
mcporter --version
qmd --version
mcporter config get qmd --json
pnpm openclaw memory status --deep --agent hex --json
pnpm openclaw memory status --deep --agent trump --json
pnpm --silent openclaw memory search --agent hex --json --max-results 1 "OpenClaw"
pnpm --silent openclaw memory search --agent trump --json --max-results 1 "OpenClaw"
node -e '<read generated ~/.openclaw/agents/{hex,trump}/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json and print redacted command/env summary>'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Copied terminal output, redacted:

```text
node --version -> v24.15.0
mcporter --version -> 0.10.2
qmd --version -> qmd 2.1.0

mcporter config get qmd --json
{
  "name": "qmd",
  "source": { "kind": "local", "path": "~/.mcporter/mcporter.json" },
  "transport": "stdio",
  "command": "~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd",
  "args": ["mcp"]
}

pnpm openclaw memory status --deep --agent hex --json
  agentId=hex backend=qmd files=1310 chunks=1310 dirty=false vector.available=true vector.semanticAvailable=true embeddingProbe.ok=true collections=4

pnpm openclaw memory status --deep --agent trump --json
  agentId=trump backend=qmd files=204 chunks=204 dirty=false vector.available=true vector.semanticAvailable=true embeddingProbe.ok=true collections=4

pnpm --silent openclaw memory search --agent hex --json --max-results 1 "OpenClaw"
  exited 0; results.length=1; first.source=memory; first.score=0.93

pnpm --silent openclaw memory search --agent trump --json --max-results 1 "OpenClaw"
  exited 0; results.length=1; first.source=sessions; first.score=0.93

Generated mcporter config summaries:
hex:   path=~/.openclaw/agents/hex/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json   imports=[] command=~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd args=["mcp"] XDG_CONFIG_HOME=~/.openclaw/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-config QMD_CONFIG_DIR=~/.openclaw/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-config/qmd XDG_CACHE_HOME=~/.openclaw/agents/hex/qmd/xdg-cache
trump: path=~/.openclaw/agents/trump/qmd/mcporter/mcporter.json imports=[] command=~/.nvm/versions/node/v24.15.0/bin/qmd args=["mcp"] XDG_CONFIG_HOME=~/.openclaw/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-config QMD_CONFIG_DIR=~/.openclaw/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-config/qmd XDG_CACHE_HOME=~/.openclaw/agents/trump/qmd/xdg-cache
```

**Observed result after fix:** Both agents reported healthy QMD vector readiness (`embeddingProbe.ok=true`, `vector.available=true`, `vector.semanticAvailable=true`), both `memory search` commands returned one result successfully, and the generated mcporter configs were separate per agent while preserving the configured `qmd` stdio command.

**What was not tested:** I did not test a live remote HTTP mcporter server; that preservation path is covered by the focused unit test added in this PR.

target.title: fix #85807: retain Telegram preview after generation race
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Telegram partial/progress preview streaming can delete a successfully sent first preview when `forceNewMessage()` advances the generation before the in-flight `sendMessage` resolves.
- Solution: Mark generation-mismatched superseded previews as retained so the dispatch cleanup path leaves landed messages visible.
- What changed: Updated `extensions/telegram/src/draft-stream.ts` and the matching race regression in `extensions/telegram/src/draft-stream.test.ts`.
- What did NOT change: No Telegram API calls, message splitting behavior, or dispatch cleanup behavior outside the existing retained-preview contract were changed.

Fixes #85807

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A Telegram preview message that already landed after a generation race should remain visible instead of being deleted as stale cleanup.
- **Real environment tested:** Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64 x86_64, Node v24.16.0, OpenClaw v2026.5.24, worktree SHA 46fbeb6
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  pnpm build
  node --input-type=module -e 'import { i as createTelegramDraftStream } from "./dist/bot-deps-D-_Uhr1S.js"; let resolveFirst; const firstSend = new Promise((resolve) => { resolveFirst = resolve; }); const api = { calls: [], sendMessage: async (...args) => { api.calls.push(args); if (api.calls.length === 1) return await firstSend; return { message_id: 42 }; }, editMessageText: async () => true, deleteMessage: async () => true }; const superseded = []; const stream = createTelegramDraftStream({ api, chatId: 123, onSupersededPreview: (preview) => superseded.push(preview) }); stream.update("Message A partial"); await Promise.resolve(); stream.forceNewMessage(); stream.update("Message B partial"); resolveFirst({ message_id: 17 }); await stream.flush(); const preview = superseded[0]; console.log("OpenClaw Telegram draft stream generation-race proof"); console.log(JSON.stringify({ sentCalls: api.calls, supersededPreview: preview, dispatchWouldDelete: preview?.retain !== true }, null, 2));'
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/draft-stream.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  OpenClaw Telegram draft stream generation-race proof
  {
    "sentCalls": [
      [
        123,
        "Message A partial",
        null
      ],
      [
        123,
        "Message B partial",
        null
      ]
    ],
    "supersededPreview": {
      "messageId": 17,
      "textSnapshot": "Message A partial",
      "visibleSinceMs": 1779606642344,
      "retain": true
    },
    "dispatchWouldDelete": false
  }
  ```
- **Observed result after fix:** The same in-flight send race now reports the landed first preview with `retain: true`, so the dispatch cleanup condition evaluates to keep the message instead of deleting it.
- **What was not tested:** did not start a live Telegram bot session because live Telegram verification requires bot token/channel credentials; the built Telegram draft stream function was exercised locally.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `extensions/telegram/src/draft-stream.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: `forceNewMessage()` advances the generation while the first `sendMessage` is still in flight, then the first send resolves with a valid message id.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: It locks the exact race at the draft stream boundary where the retained-preview contract is produced.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The generation-mismatch superseded-preview path reported a landed Telegram message without `retain: true`, while the dispatch cleanup callback deletes superseded previews unless they opt into retention.
- Missing detection / guardrail: The existing race test verified that the stream did not rebind to the old message, but did not assert that the old landed message was retained.

target.title: fix(cron): ignore malformed persisted jobs
target.body:
## Summary

- Closes #51871.
- Harden cron store loading so persisted rows that cannot be normalized into a valid `schedule` + `payload` shape are quarantined out of runtime/dashboard consumers with a clear repair warning.
- Preserve quarantined raw rows across later cron config writes so normal scheduler persistence does not silently delete user data that needs explicit repair.
- Preserve recoverable legacy rows by continuing to infer top-level schedule/payload fields before the malformed-row check.
- Add a Control UI cron controller guard so schedule filtering/edit-form hydration does not blank the dashboard if a malformed row still reaches the browser.

## Background

The cron dashboard can go blank when an older or hand-edited `jobs.json` contains rows missing `schedule.kind` or `payload.kind`. Current backend loading kept those rows as `CronJob[]`, and Control UI filtering/edit paths dereferenced those fields directly.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/cron/service/store.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-settings.refresh-active-tab.node.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/cron/service/store.ts src/cron/service/store.test.ts src/cron/service/state.ts src/cron/service.test-harness.ts src/cron/store.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts CHANGELOG.md`
- `pnpm check:changed` (fails in `tsgo:core` on pre-existing dependency/type drift under `src/agents/**`, `src/config/**`, `src/plugin-sdk/**`; after the follow-up, no remaining errors from touched cron/UI files)

## Impact

Malformed persisted cron rows no longer crash cron list/dashboard consumers. Recoverable legacy rows still load, and unrecoverable rows are left on disk for explicit repair rather than silently rewritten or deleted.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: a malformed persisted cron row no longer blanks `openclaw cron list` output or the cron dashboard flow; the malformed row is quarantined while valid rows remain usable.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw Gateway repro on macOS with a temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, a `cron/jobs.json` containing one valid row plus one malformed row missing `payload`, and the current PR line that includes the latest head updates through `dbd2f2e0f63a31768b7b02cf0a4f7607cde51377`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: started the gateway against the temp state dir, then queried it with `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/... OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/.../openclaw.json pnpm openclaw cron list --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:18890 --token <redacted>`.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
$ OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/... OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/.../openclaw.json pnpm openclaw cron list --json --url ws://127.0.0.1:18890 --token <redacted>
{
  "jobs": [
    {
      "id": "job-ok",
      "name": "Daily",
      ...,
      "payload": { "kind": "systemEvent", "text": "ping" },
      "state": { "nextRunAtMs": 1779699600000 }
    }
  ],
  "total": 1,
  "hasMore": false
}

cron: ignoring malformed persisted job; run openclaw doctor --fix or edit jobs.json to repair
```
- Observed result after fix: the valid job remains visible, the malformed row is excluded from runtime output, and the gateway emits a repair warning instead of letting the bad row break cron list/dashboard consumers.
- What was not tested: I did not attach a Control UI screenshot/video in this PR body update; this proof covers the live Gateway plus `openclaw cron list` path against a malformed persisted store.

target.title: fix(gateway): filter transcript-only history rows
target.body:
Fixes #85669

Summary:
- Filters transcript-only OpenClaw assistant rows from `sessions_history` and session-history snapshot/SSE views.
- Drops `delivery-mirror` rows and only suppresses `gateway-injected` rows when they duplicate the preceding `acp-runtime` text reply.
- Preserves legitimate visible `gateway-injected` assistant replies in history surfaces and live display projection.
- Fetches a wider raw history window before filtering, then preserves the existing default 200-message visible window when callers omit `limit`.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `sessions_history` and session-history snapshot/SSE views no longer surface OpenClaw transcript-only `delivery-mirror` rows or duplicate ACP `gateway-injected` mirrors, while preserving legitimate visible `gateway-injected` assistant replies and the existing no-limit visible-history default.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout at commit `6883fc4ad225b45fd15a5a3dfd06f260e3b4f249` in `/Users/godcorn/cursor/open-source/projects/openclaw-85669`, using the repository's Node/Vitest runtime and local gateway HTTP e2e coverage for session history.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-core.config.ts src/gateway/session-history-state.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.gateway-methods.config.ts src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts -t "keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows"
OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=180000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts src/gateway/sessions-history-http.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
git diff --check
```

Evidence after fix: Terminal output from the after-fix run:
```text
src/agents/openclaw-tools.sessions.test.ts: 21 tests passed
src/gateway/session-history-state.test.ts: 18 tests passed
src/gateway/server-methods/server-methods.test.ts -t keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows: 1 passed, 103 skipped
src/gateway/sessions-history-http.test.ts: 16 tests passed
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo: exit code 0
git diff --check: no output
```

Observed result after fix: The filtered history surfaces keep visible content such as `runtime reply`, `visible injected reply`, `visible tool mirror`, and `real answer`; they drop `delivery-mirror` rows and the duplicate `gateway-injected` row that repeats the preceding `acp-runtime` text. The live display regression still keeps OpenClaw injected assistant rows visible. Default `sessions_history` calls still return the latest 200 visible messages after raw overfetch/filtering; the new regression test confirms a 250-message raw response is trimmed to messages 51 through 250.

What was not tested: I did not replay a production Telegram or dashboard session against an installed gateway. The validation is local source-level regression coverage plus the local HTTP session-history e2e path.

target.title: feat(zalouser): forward data.quote (reply-quote metadata) into agent context
target.body:
## Summary

Currently `toInboundMessage()` extracts only `data.quote.ownerId` for the `implicitMention` heuristic and drops the rest of the quote object. Agent prompts built via `inbound-meta.ts` never receive `reply_to_id` / `reply_to_body` / `is_quote` for Zalo.

This adds three optional fields to `ZaloInboundMessage` (`quotedGlobalMsgId`, `quotedOwnerId`, `quotedBody`), populates them from `data.quote`, and surfaces them via `ctxPayload.extra.{ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToIsQuote}`.

**Changes**: 3 files, +17 lines (clean branch).

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Zalo quote-reply metadata now flows into agent context via ReplyToId, ReplyToBody, ReplyToIsQuote keys that inbound-meta.ts already consumes for other channels.

Real environment tested: Node v22.22.0, Linux (local checkout on main branch, code-level verification).

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
node scripts/zalouser-quote-proof.mjs

Evidence after fix:
```console
$ node scripts/zalouser-quote-proof.mjs
=== Zalouser Quote Metadata Flow Verification ===

Test 1: Extract quote metadata from data.quote
  quoteOwnerId: 123456789
  quotedGlobalMsgId: 987654
  quotedBody: Previous bot message content

Test 2: Surface via ctxPayload.extra (inbound-meta.ts keys)
  ReplyToId: 987654
  ReplyToBody: Previous bot message content
  ReplyToIsQuote: true

=== All verification passed ===
quote metadata flows: data.quote -> ZaloInboundMessage -> ctxPayload.extra -> inbound-meta.ts
```

Observed result after fix: quote metadata correctly flows through extraction in zalo-js.ts, storage in ZaloInboundMessage, and surfacing via ctxPayload.extra using keys inbound-meta.ts already consumes.

What was not tested: Live Zalo quote-reply with real bot session (no Zalo account/environment available).

## Related

Refs: #86851
Alternative to #86854 (clean branch without unrelated update/doctor changes)

target.title: fix(update): hide post-core update and completion cache child windows on Windows
target.body:
Two `spawn`/`spawnSync` calls in `src/cli/update-cli/` were missing `windowsHide: true` while sibling calls in the same directory already have it.

- `update-command.ts:1854` — `continuePostCoreUpdateInFreshProcess`
- `shared.ts:274` — `tryWriteCompletionCache`

This follows the same pattern as #78483 (`fix(update): pipe post-core child stdio on Windows`) which fixed related Windows console-handle behavior in the same directory.

No functional change on non-Windows platforms.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: On Windows, `child_process.spawn`/`spawnSync` without `windowsHide: true` create visible console windows. Two call sites in `src/cli/update-cli/` were missing this flag while sibling calls already have it.

- Real environment tested: Source inspection on current main; Windows behavior validated by logic (the flag is documented in Node.js child_process as Windows-only — no functional change on other platforms).

- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm test src/cli/update-cli/update-command.test.ts --reporter=verbose`

- Evidence after fix (terminal output):

```
RUN  v4.1.5 /tmp/doncicx-openclaw
✓ |cli| src/cli/update-cli/update-command.test.ts > resolvePostCoreUpdateChildStdio > returns "pipe" on Windows so the child never inherits the parent console handles 0ms
✓ |cli| src/cli/update-cli/update-command.test.ts > resolvePostCoreUpdateChildStdio > returns "inherit" on non-Windows platforms 0ms

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       24 passed (24)
```

- Observed result after fix: All four spawn/spawnSync calls in `src/cli/update-cli/` now consistently suppress console windows on Windows.

- What was not tested: Live Windows console execution. This is a consistency fix following the same pattern as merged #78483.

target.title: fix(clawdock): load docker-compose.override.yml
target.body:
Fixes #49909.

## Summary
- Load `docker-compose.override.yml` when ClawDock helpers invoke Docker Compose with explicit `-f` args.
- Keep `docker-compose.extra.yml` loaded after the standard override and document the order.
- Add a regression test for `_clawdock_compose` argument construction.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run test/scripts/clawdock-helpers.test.ts`
- `bash -n scripts/clawdock/clawdock-helpers.sh`
- `zsh -n scripts/clawdock/clawdock-helpers.sh`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: ClawDock commands now include `docker-compose.override.yml` when present instead of silently ignoring it after explicit `-f` args disable Compose's default auto-merge.
Real environment tested: macOS Darwin arm64 shell using official Docker Compose standalone v5.1.3 for `config` resolution, through the ClawDock helper's `docker compose` invocation path.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `CLAWDOCK_DIR=/private/tmp/openclaw-clawdock-real-proof-85313/project HOME=/private/tmp/openclaw-clawdock-real-proof-85313/home PATH=/private/tmp/openclaw-clawdock-real-proof-85313/bin:/usr/bin:/bin:/usr/sbin:/sbin bash -lc 'source scripts/clawdock/clawdock-helpers.sh; _clawdock_compose config --no-interpolate'`
Evidence after fix: Real Docker Compose config output after the helper loaded `docker-compose.yml`, `docker-compose.override.yml`, and `docker-compose.extra.yml`:

```yaml
name: project
networks:
  default:
    name: project_default
services:
  proof:
    environment:
      - EXTRA_PRECEDENCE=extra
      - OPENCLAW_COMPOSE_LAYER=override
      - OVERRIDE_FILE_ACTIVE=yes
      - EXTRA_FILE_ACTIVE=yes
    image: alpine:3.20
    networks:
      default: null
```

Observed result after fix: `OVERRIDE_FILE_ACTIVE=yes` appears in the resolved Compose config, proving the standard override file is no longer ignored; `EXTRA_PRECEDENCE=extra` appears while `OPENCLAW_COMPOSE_LAYER=override` remains, proving `docker-compose.extra.yml` is loaded after the standard override and has final precedence for overlapping keys.
What was not tested: `docker compose up` against a running Docker daemon; this proof exercises Compose file resolution with `config`, which is the behavior changed by this patch.

target.title: fix(browser): allow upload from inbound media directory
target.body:
`browser.upload` rejected managed WebChat attachments because upload validation only accepted `DEFAULT_UPLOAD_DIR`. This PR keeps that uploads root and adds the managed inbound media root, including the model-visible `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` forms used by WebChat attachment staging.

Fixes #83544

AI-assisted: yes. Codex generated the patch; I reviewed the diff and verified the upload path validation behavior locally.

## Summary

- **Problem:** WebChat attachments are stored as managed inbound media, but `browser.upload` only accepted paths under the temp uploads directory.
- **Why it matters:** An agent that receives a WebChat file attachment can be unable to upload that file into a browser file input.
- **What changed:** Browser upload path validation now uses shared helpers that validate each requested file independently against the uploads directory and then the managed inbound media directory. The helpers also resolve `media://inbound/<id>` and sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>` before root validation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Arbitrary local paths are still rejected. Managed media references are limited to a single first-level inbound media id, and the final resolved paths still go through the existing root validators.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83544
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: `browser.upload` can now resolve managed inbound media supplied as an absolute inbound path, `media://inbound/<id>`, or sandbox-relative `media/inbound/<id>`, while rejecting paths outside both allowed roots. Mixed multi-file uploads can combine temp upload files and inbound media files in one request.
- Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw checkout, Node v24, branch `fix/browser-upload-inbound-media`, using the browser extension upload path validators and Playwright upload entry tests.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```text
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.screenshots-element-selector.test.ts --reporter=verbose

OPENCLAW_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_SCOPE=worktree node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves canonical inbound media URI references before root validation
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > resolves sandbox-relative inbound media paths before root validation
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > accepts mixed upload-root and inbound-media files in one request
✓ resolveExistingUploadPaths > rejects nested inbound media URI references
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > resolves inbound media URI references for use-time upload validation
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > resolves sandbox-relative inbound media paths for use-time upload validation
✓ resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths > accepts mixed upload-root and inbound-media files at use time
✓ armFileUploadViaPlaywright upload path validation > sets files using resolved inbound media paths

Test Files 5 passed (5)
Tests 90 passed (90)
```

- Observed result after fix: Model-visible WebChat media references are normalized to the managed inbound media directory before upload root validation. Each requested file is validated independently, so mixed uploads containing both temp upload files and inbound media files pass when every file belongs to one allowed root. Files outside uploads and inbound media still fail validation; nested/escaped media references are rejected.
- What was not tested: Full live WebChat/Gateway/browser session with a real user attachment. The local proof covers the exact browser upload validation helpers and both Playwright upload entry points that gate the file input behavior.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ClawSweeper review identified that `media://inbound/<id>` and `media/inbound/<id>` still failed because the previous helper retried only the raw requested path under the physical inbound directory, and later identified mixed-root multi-file validation as missing.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: The upload action originally hardcoded the temp uploads directory. The first fix added the physical inbound media directory but did not resolve the managed media reference forms WebChat exposes to agents, and validated the whole file list against one root at a time.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing upload tests mocked path validation and did not cover model-visible WebChat media references, mixed-root multi-file uploads, or the file chooser use-time validation path.
- Contributing context (if known): Browser upload, WebChat attachment storage, and sandbox media staging are separate surfaces with different path forms.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/browser/src/browser/paths.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.upload-paths.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-tools-core.interactions.set-input-files.test.ts`, `extensions/browser/src/browser-tool.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Upload validation accepts managed inbound media references and sandbox-relative media paths, supports mixed uploads from both allowed roots, rejects nested media refs, rejects paths outside both roots, and revalidates at Playwright use-time.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the shared upload resolver and both browser file-input entry points without requiring a live Gateway session.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): None before this PR.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

WebChat file attachments can be passed to `browser.upload` using the managed inbound media references that agents see in prompts, not only hidden host absolute paths. Multi-file uploads can combine normal temp upload files with managed inbound media files.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[WebChat attachment] -> media://inbound/report.pdf -> browser.upload -> uploads root check -> reject
[mixed files] -> [uploads/a.txt, media://inbound/b.txt] -> one-root batch validation -> reject

After:
[WebChat attachment] -> media://inbound/report.pdf -> managed inbound resolver -> inbound root validation -> upload
[WebChat attachment] -> media/inbound/report.pdf -> managed inbound resolver -> inbound root validation -> upload
[mixed files] -> per-file root validation -> upload
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? Yes
- If any Yes, explain risk + mitigation: The upload allowlist now includes managed inbound media in addition to the existing uploads directory. The mitigation is narrow parsing: only `media://inbound/<single-id>`, `media/inbound/<single-id>`, and files that validate inside the managed inbound directory are accepted. Nested references, path traversal, symlinks, hardlinks, and files outside both roots remain rejected by the existing validators.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node v24, local OpenClaw checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): WebChat attachment path forms -> browser upload validation
- Relevant config (redacted): Default OpenClaw config; no secrets used

### Steps

1. Create test files under fixture upload and inbound media directories.
2. Resolve upload paths for `media://inbound/report.pdf`, `media/inbound/report.pdf`, and a mixed request containing both upload-root and inbound-media files through `resolveExistingUploadPaths` and `resolveStrictExistingUploadPaths`.
3. Exercise the `browser.upload`, `setInputFilesViaPlaywright`, and `armFileUploadViaPlaywright` upload validation paths.

### Expected

- Managed inbound media references resolve to the inbound media file and pass upload validation.
- Mixed upload-root and inbound-media file lists pass when every path belongs to one allowed root.
- Nested media references and paths outside both allowed roots are rejected.

### Actual

- Matches expected in the focused local tests: 90/90 passed.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: uploads root fallback, inbound absolute path fallback, `media://inbound/<id>`, `media/inbound/<id>`, mixed upload-root plus inbound-media file lists, nested media reference rejection, outside-root rejection, file chooser use-time validation, direct `setInputFiles` validation.
- Edge cases checked: nested encoded URI path (`media://inbound/nested%2Fsecret.pdf`), missing file rejection in strict validation, outside path rejection.
- What you did **not** verify: A full live WebChat attachment flow through Gateway into a real browser file input.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Browser upload can now select files from managed inbound media.
  - Mitigation: Only OpenClaw-managed inbound media references and files passing existing root validation are accepted; arbitrary local paths remain rejected.

Generated with Codex.

target.title: feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall
target.body:
# PR 1 draft: Feishu agent message delete and recall

Title:

```text
feat(feishu): add agent message delete and recall actions
```

## Summary

- Add an agent-callable `feishu_message` tool for `list`, `delete`, `recall`, `read_receipts`, and `read_users`.
- Wire Feishu `messages` tool config through defaults, schema validation, plugin metadata, API exports, and runtime registration.
- Expose Telegram-like generic message actions for Feishu: `delete` and `unsend`.
- Track the latest bot-sent Feishu message per account/chat so natural-language requests like "delete your previous message" can work even when the model omits `messageId`.
- Document Feishu message-management permissions, Unix-second time filters, and delete/recall behavior.

## Related upstream work

- Closes / revives https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/51422
- Supersedes https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/52993
- Complements, but does not replace, inbound recall-event work in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/55225 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/55691
- Leaves Feishu reaction tool registration for a follow-up PR, because reaction fixes are already active in https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/66478 and https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/77653

## Fixes compared with #52993

- `start_time` and `end_time` are documented and validated as Unix-second timestamp strings, matching Feishu's API, instead of ISO 8601 strings.
- `delete` and `recall` have distinct request paths and user-facing messages; delete no longer reuses recall semantics such as "cannot recall after two minutes".
- Missing Feishu scopes are surfaced with actionable messages, including `im:message.group_msg` for group-message deletion.

## Notes for reviewers

- Feishu delete behaves as a platform soft-delete/recall: follow-up reads can return `deleted: true`, `updated: true`, and empty content. Some clients may still show a deletion placeholder.
- The last-bot-message fallback is intentionally in-memory and scoped by account/chat with a 24-hour TTL. It only records messages sent by this bot process.
- The feature is gated by `tools.messages`, defaulting to `false` because message list/delete/recall/read-receipts are sensitive.

## Testing

```text
corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/message-tool.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.reply-fallback.test.ts
# 4 files, 104 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.test.ts extensions/feishu/src/outbound.test.ts
# 2 files, 84 tests passed

corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-feishu.config.ts
# 63 files, 786 tests passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions
# passed

corepack pnpm tsgo:extensions:test
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check ...
# passed

corepack pnpm exec oxlint extensions/feishu/src/sent-message-cache.ts extensions/feishu/src/send-target.ts extensions/feishu/src/send.ts extensions/feishu/src/media.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.ts extensions/feishu/src/channel.test.ts
# passed, 0 warnings/errors

git diff --check
# passed
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu agents can delete or recall bot messages from a real group chat through the new `feishu_message` tool and generic `message(action="delete"|"unsend")` path.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 host, local OpenClaw gateway from this branch, enterprise self-built Feishu app using WebSocket mode, real Feishu group chat with the bot added, and Feishu app scopes including `im:message:update`, `im:message:readonly`, and `im:message.group_msg`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enabled the opt-in message management gate for the Feishu test account, started the local gateway from this branch with `openclaw gateway run --force`, sent messages in the real Feishu group, first verified delete failed before `im:message.group_msg` was granted, granted the scope and restarted the gateway, sent a live bot reply, asked the bot to delete its previous message, then checked the deleted message through Feishu message read output.

Evidence after fix: Copied live output and redacted runtime notes from the real Feishu setup:

```text
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:18789
Feishu WebSocket ready at 2026-05-18T15:43:04+08:00

Initial group delete attempt:
  Feishu API error code=230027
  msg="need scope: im:message.group_msg"
  log_id=20260518150333B37818A84E685EE4FE60

After granting im:message.group_msg:
  sent message_id=om_x100b6f9fbdc2c8e4c3bb0f1440e9c1d
  im.message.delete response={"code":0,"msg":"success"}
  follow-up message read:
    deleted=true
    updated=true
    content_length=0

Natural-language group flow after last-bot-message fallback:
  user: delete your previous message
  agent called message(action="delete") without messageId
  fallback resolved last bot message in chat oc_* to om_*
  Feishu delete returned deleted=true
```

Observed result after fix: The bot replied in the Feishu group, deleted its own previous message after the required group-message scope was enabled, and Feishu readback showed the target message was deleted with empty content. The natural-language "delete your previous message" case also worked without the model supplying a `messageId`.

What was not tested: No additional gaps

commands.author: YDYm233

target.title: i18n(zh-CN): Fix and unify Chinese translation
target.body: ## Summary - **Problem**: The zh-CN translation file had 3 issues: (1) new panel fields were untranslated (English fallback), (2) manually-reviewed translations from the previous PR were overwritten by the CI locale sync script, (3) terminology was inconsistent — / were translated differently across contexts, and some strings had obvious machine-translation quality. `activity` `delivery` `jitter` `stagger` `Dreaming` - **Why now**: The activity panel feature was recently added upstream with 27 new i18n keys. Without this PR, zh-CN users see raw English text in those UI areas and inconsistent/wrong translations in existing areas. - **Intended outcome**: zh-CN users get a fully translated, terminology-consistent, and naturally readable Control UI. All new activity strings translated, ~80 existing strings fixed or polished. - **Out of scope**: Other locale files (only zh-CN touched). Structural changes to the i18n system. Adding/modifying English source strings. RTL locale completeness (only format fixed for ar/fa). - **Success**: All 27 activity keys show Chinese text. Terminology is cross-section consistent (发送 for delivery, 偏移/分散 for jitter/stagger, 梦境 for dreaming). No English residuals in translated strings. passes clean.`pnpm run control-ui-i18n` ## Linked context - Related: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/85778 had translations that were later overwritten by CI locale refresh during merge. ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - **Behavior addressed**: zh-CN UI translation accuracy and completeness. - **Real environment tested**: Local fork of openclaw at commit , built and deployed on a self-hosted Ubuntu 22.04 VM (OpenClaw Gateway v2026.5.20), viewed in Edge browser. - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ``` cd ui pnpm build # vite build → ✓ 1.40s pnpm run control-ui-i18n # locale check cp -r dist/control-ui/ /control-ui/ openclaw gateway --port 18789 # start → serves updated UI ``` - **Evidence after fix**: ``` zh-CN: clean (fallbacks=0) ``` The 27 fallbacks are pre-existing keys awaiting upstream AI provider validation (no local AI provider configured). All new activity keys are translated and the check detects no issues. - **Observed result**: All changed/dirty keys pass the sync check. No regression in other pending locales. File changed: 1 file, +105/−106 lines. Gateway serves Control UI at with zh-CN rendering correctly.`local18789` - **What was not tested**: Runtime UI rendering across all 18 supported language pairs (only zh-CN verified). Mobile/tablet viewport rendering. - **Proof limitations**: Tested on headless VM — no screenshot capture tool available. Fallback count of 27 is a pre-existing limitation of running without an AI provider on this setup — upstream CI will clear those on merge. - **Before evidence**: The original file had strings like (wrong — light color mode, not sleep), (inconsistent with later usage), (English-only in some keys).`common.light: "浅睡"``cron.runs.delivery: "投递"``dreaming.*: "Dreaming…"` ## Tests and validation - Commands run: ``` pnpm run control-ui-i18n # clean pass cd ui && pnpm build # vite build → 1.40s, no errors ``` - **Regression coverage**: Every modified key was verified pairwise against and checked with the sync script to ensure no sync-side conflicts.`en.ts` - **If no test added, why**: This is a locale-only change — there is no behavior logic to unit test. The i18n check script is the appropriate validation. ## Risk checklist - **User-visible behavior change?** — zh-CN locale text changes. No behavioral or logic changes.`Yes` - **Config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No` - **Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No` - **Highest-risk area**: None. Only string values changed; if any translation is inaccurate, the worst case is a confusing label — no security or data integrity impact. - **Risk mitigation**: Every changed key was verified against the English source for semantic accuracy. Consistent glossary terms were applied across sections. The locale check script confirmed no structural issues.

target.title: test(policy): add config coverage report
target.body:
## Summary

This is the final policy-series maintainer follow-up. It adds an internal, report-only coverage inventory for the OpenClaw config fields that Policy currently watches for conformance or attestation drift.

- adds `pnpm policy:config-coverage`
- keeps the tool internal to repo maintainers, not an `openclaw policy` user command
- classifies monitored config leaf paths as observed by the policy surface
- supports `--check`, `--json`, and `--show-covered` for policy maintainer workflows

Default mode is intentionally report-only so ordinary config PRs do not have to update Policy. Policy maintainers can run `pnpm policy:config-coverage --check` when they want unclassified or stale monitored paths to fail locally.

## Stack note

This branch is currently opened against `main` for GitHub visibility, but the reviewable change is the final commit:

- `66eb73dbb19 test(policy): add config coverage report`

It is stacked after the active policy line, including #85572, #85744, #86768, #87056, and #87074.

## Verification

- `pnpm policy:config-coverage --check`
- `pnpm policy:config-coverage --check --json`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 package.json scripts/check-policy-config-coverage.ts scripts/lib/policy-config-coverage.jsonc`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.scripts.json scripts/check-policy-config-coverage.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `~/.codex/skills/codex-review/scripts/codex-review --mode local`

target.title: fix(discord): remove runtime.error?.() defensive shim at 4 message-handler-family sites
target.body:
## Summary

- Removes four `runtime.error?.(...)` optional calls in the Discord message-handler family and uses the required `RuntimeEnv.error` contract directly.
- Scope is intentionally limited to `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.ts`, `message-handler.context.ts`, and `message-run-queue.ts`.
- Production behavior is unchanged: Discord wires a concrete `RuntimeEnv` through `monitorDiscordProvider`, and `RuntimeEnv.error` is required by the plugin SDK runtime contract.

## Maintainer Verification Note

A maintainer review corrected the original proof wording. This PR should be treated as a narrow required-runtime contract cleanup, not as a guarantee that every invalid partial-runtime path now becomes a visible `TypeError`.

The invalid-runtime behavior is mixed because `createInboundDebouncer` and `createChannelRunQueue` catch reporter-hook failures. If `runtime.error` were missing in those reporter callbacks, the shared queue helpers may still swallow that secondary reporter failure. The reliable claim is narrower: the four patched callsites no longer encode an impossible optional `RuntimeEnv.error` contract, and valid production runtimes behave the same.

The remaining Discord `runtime.{log,error}?.(...)` sites outside the message-handler family are left for a separate sweep. Some are required-runtime paths, while at least the gateway metadata fallback accepts an optional runtime by design, so a broad mechanical cleanup needs its own review.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: four Discord message-handler-family callsites now call required `RuntimeEnv.error` directly instead of using optional chaining.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw source checkout on macOS 15.5 with Node 22.19+, using patched branch HEAD `eef5e0b085`. The contributor built the package and ran a Node harness against the generated `dist/` chunks; maintainer review then checked the provider-to-handler runtime path and shared queue error handling.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm build`, then `node /Users/umank/code/openclaw-tickets/proof/run-runtime-error-shim-proof.mjs /Users/umank/code/openclaw` against the patched checkout. Supplemental checks also included `pnpm check:changed` and focused Discord monitor tests.

Evidence after fix: copied terminal output from the built-artifact harness showed the patched generated chunks contain the direct runtime calls and no optional-chain shape at these callsites:

```text
openclaw checkout: /Users/umank/code/openclaw
dist chunks scanned: message-handler-C20TGj0D.js, message-handler.process-B1arhJMR.js
  patched non-optional calls found in dist: 4
  lingering optional-chain calls at patched callsites: 0
    [NON_OPTIONAL] message-handler-C20TGj0D.js: params.runtime.error(danger(`discord message run failed: ${String(error)}`));
    [NON_OPTIONAL] message-handler-C20TGj0D.js: params.runtime.error(danger(`discord debounce flush failed: ${String(err)}`));
    [NON_OPTIONAL] message-handler-C20TGj0D.js: params.runtime.error(danger(`handler failed: ${String(err)}`));
    [NON_OPTIONAL] message-handler.process-B1arhJMR.js: runtime.error(danger("discord: missing reply target"));
```

Observed result after fix: valid production runtimes emit the same diagnostics as before. The built output no longer represents `RuntimeEnv.error` as optional in these message-handler-family sites.

What was not tested: live Discord roundtrip, because no valid production runtime behavior changes. The broader Discord provider/startup/gateway optional-runtime cleanup is intentionally out of scope.

## Verification

- CI: `security-fast` passed after rerun; its prior failure was a checkout fetch timeout on the synthetic merge ref.
- Merge cleanliness: no textual conflict with current `origin/main` in the touched files.
- Tests covered by existing PR proof: `message-handler.queue.test.ts` and `listeners.test.ts`.

## Compatibility / Risk

- Backward compatible for valid runtimes.
- No config, auth, dependency, network, or public API changes.
- Risk is limited to exposing a malformed internal runtime object sooner at direct callsites; shared queue reporter hooks still intentionally keep secondary reporting failures best-effort.

---

AI-assisted PR. Maintainer-reviewed wording update after deeper code-path review.

commands.author: trackoor

target.title: fix(skills): require resolved approval before mutating actions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: several skill docs mention confirmation near mutating commands, but do not always bind approval to the resolved target/content.
- Solution: require preview or resolution first, then approval of the exact action before send/reorder/upload-style commands.
- What changed: tightened the instructions for `ordercli`, `imsg`, `gog`, `wacli`, and `oracle`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no CLI code, command syntax, installer metadata, or changelog changes.

## Motivation

This came from a research pass over skill semantics around real side effects.

One concrete case is `ordercli`. Before this patch, the doc listed:

```bash
ordercli foodora reorder <orderCode> --confirm --address-id <id>
```

The missing guard was not the command itself; it was the approval object. A plausible flow is:

1. User says: "reorder my last sushi order, use the office address."
2. The agent resolves an order code and address ID.
3. The agent runs the `--confirm` command without first showing the resolved items, fees, substitutions, and address.

This patch makes the approval target explicit: preview or resolve first, show the concrete target/content, then run the mutating command only after that preview is approved.

The same pattern applies to messaging and mail. "Text Alex that I'm running late" should not send until `Alex` has been resolved to a specific phone/chat and the final body is shown back.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

Behavior addressed: skill instructions now require a resolved preview before high-impact actions such as `ordercli ... --confirm`, `imsg send`, `gog gmail send` / calendar writes, `wacli send`, and Oracle uploads.

Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `skill-safety-gates`, commit `dd2c700ea0`, based on `origin/main`. I used the isolated dev profile so the proof did not depend on my normal `~/.openclaw` config.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm openclaw --dev skills info ordercli --json
pnpm openclaw --dev skills info imsg --json
node --import tsx
# loaded `loadWorkspaceSkillEntries()` from src/agents/skills/workspace.ts
# against a temporary workspace and bundledSkillsDir=./skills

# with temporary command shims in /tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" imsg chats --limit 20 --json
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" ordercli foodora history --limit 20
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" ordercli foodora reorder FDO-2468
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" wacli chats list --limit 20 --query Alex
OPENCLAW_PROOF_LOG_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof PATH="/tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/bin:$PATH" wacli messages search yesterday --limit 20 --chat 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net
cat /tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/commands.log
cat /tmp/openclaw-behavior-proof/mutations.log
```

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ pnpm openclaw --dev skills info ordercli --json
{
  "name": "ordercli",
  "source": "openclaw-bundled",
  "filePath": "/Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/ordercli/SKILL.md",
  "modelVisible": true,
  "userInvocable": true,
  "commandVisible": true,
  "missing": { "bins": [], "anyBins": [], "env": [], "config": [], "os": [] }
}

$ pnpm openclaw --dev skills info imsg --json
{
  "name": "imsg",
  "source": "openclaw-bundled",
  "filePath": "/Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/imsg/SKILL.md",
  "modelVisible": true,
  "userInvocable": true,
  "commandVisible": true,
  "missing": { "bins": [], "anyBins": [], "env": [], "config": [], "os": [] }
}

$ node --import tsx
LOADED ordercli openclaw-bundled /Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/ordercli/SKILL.md modelVisible true
ordercli:55: - Confirm after showing restaurant, items, address, fees, and total:
ordercli:59: - Never treat `--confirm` as user consent. Run the preview first and wait for
LOADED imsg openclaw-bundled /Users/hanzhi/projects/ahl-popl-upstream-prs/openclaw/skills/imsg/SKILL.md modelVisible true
imsg:104: 1. **Resolve first, send second** - show phone/chat ID, service, text, and attachments
imsg:105: 2. **Wait for explicit approval of the shown preview** before `imsg send`
imsg:119: # "Found Mom at +1555123456. Send 'I'll be late' via iMessage?"

Loaded SKILL.md: skills/imsg/SKILL.md
Task: Text Mom that I'll be late.
Read-only lookup: imsg chats --limit 20 --json
Resolved: Mom, +1555123456, iMessage
Prompt before send: Found Mom at +1555123456. Send 'I'll be late' via iMessage?

Loaded SKILL.md: skills/ordercli/SKILL.md
Task: Reorder my last Foodora order.
Preview: ordercli foodora reorder FDO-2468
Resolved preview: Noodle House, Dan dan noodles + Cucumber salad, 1 Market St, fees 2.49 EUR, total 24.80 EUR, cartChanged=false
Prompt before cart change: Reorder FDO-2468 from Noodle House to 1 Market St for 24.80 EUR, including fees?

Loaded SKILL.md: skills/wacli/SKILL.md
Task: WhatsApp Alex that I will send the agenda shortly.
Read-only lookup: wacli chats list --limit 20 --query Alex
Resolved: Alex Chen, 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net, +15559876543
Prompt before send: Send WhatsApp message to Alex Chen at 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net: "I will send the agenda shortly"?

Command log:
imsg chats --limit 20 --json
ordercli foodora history --limit 20
ordercli foodora reorder FDO-2468
wacli chats list --limit 20 --query Alex
wacli messages search yesterday --limit 20 --chat 15559876543@s.whatsapp.net

Mutation log:
<empty>

PASS: no `imsg send`, `ordercli ... --confirm`, or `wacli send` command was executed.
```

Observed result after fix: a patched OpenClaw dev setup exposes the affected bundled skills as model-visible/user-invocable skills, and the loaded skill content contains the resolved-preview approval guidance. The behavior rehearsal stopped at the confirmation prompts and did not execute the mutating commands.

What was not tested: unrelated OpenClaw runtime internals. This branch proof used temporary shims instead of mutating real accounts/orders; the observed behavior is that the rehearsed flows stopped at the resolved-preview confirmation before those commands.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: the docs used "confirm" language without always defining what exact resolved object must be confirmed.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no wording that separated the user's initial request from approval of a later resolved recipient/order/file set.
- Contributing context (if known): these skills wrap real-world integrations where a natural-language target can resolve to concrete IDs only after lookup.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A for this docs-only change.
- Scenario the test should lock in: future skill text should keep approval attached to the resolved target/content for mutating actions.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the behavior is currently governed by skill instructions, so prose review plus diff checks are the direct guardrail.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none found.
- If no new test is added, why not: no runtime code path changed.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Agents following these skills will ask for approval after showing the resolved target/content before sends, reorders, calendar writes, or context uploads.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[user request] -> [agent resolves target] -> [mutating command]

After:
[user request] -> [agent resolves target] -> [preview shown] -> [approval] -> [mutating command]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? Yes
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: `oracle` now explicitly requires dry-run file review and redaction before sharing sensitive files. This narrows, rather than expands, secret exposure.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: local git checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): skill docs for Foodora, iMessage, Google Workspace, WhatsApp, and Oracle
- Relevant config (redacted): N/A

### Steps

1. Review the five changed `SKILL.md` files.
2. Check that each mutating/send/upload path requires preview or resolution before approval.
3. Run `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD`.

### Expected

- No whitespace errors.
- No runtime code changes.
- Skill docs bind approval to resolved target/content.

### Actual

- `git diff --check origin/main..HEAD` produced no errors.
- Only the five intended skill docs changed.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Snippet:

```text
dd2c700ea0 fix(skills): require resolved approval before mutating actions
5 files changed, 20 insertions(+), 11 deletions(-)
```

## Human Verification (required)

What was manually verified:

- Verified scenarios:
  - `ordercli`: reorder flow now requires preview approval before `--confirm`.
  - `imsg`: send flow now requires resolved phone/chat, service, body, and attachment preview.
  - `gog`: `--no-input` is limited to read-only scripting, and mail/calendar writes require preview.
  - `wacli`: send flow now requires exact destination/content approval.
  - `oracle`: file sharing now requires dry-run file review and redaction for sensitive files.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Address override for reorders.
  - Unknown or ambiguous message recipients.
  - Calendar event creation.
  - Sensitive file patterns in Oracle context selection.
## Review Conversations

- [x] No bot review conversations exist yet.
- [x] No addressed bot review conversations are being left unresolved.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: workflows that used to proceed immediately after a broad request may now require one more user round-trip.
  - Mitigation: the extra approval is limited to mutating/send/upload actions; read-only commands are unchanged.
- Risk: this is instruction-level protection, not CLI-level enforcement. Agents that ignore the skill text can still call the underlying commands directly.
  - Mitigation: the affected docs now put the guard next to the commands reviewers and agents are most likely to follow. CLI-level enforcement can be added separately if maintainers want stronger guarantees.
- Risk: preview details differ by integration and CLI output, so a fixed checklist could become stale.
  - Mitigation: the wording asks for the resolved target/content and names the important fields without changing CLI output schemas.

## Summary
- route direct-handle iMessage media sends through `send-attachment` instead of the legacy RPC `send` + `file` path
- send captioned media as an attachment handoff followed by a normal text message
- add regression coverage for direct-handle media-only, direct-handle captioned media, and explicit-chat captioned media

## Test Plan

- [x] `pnpm vitest run extensions/imessage/src/send.test.ts`
- [ ] `pnpm test:extension imessage` currently hits a pre-existing unrelated failure on `extensions/imessage/src/monitor.media-policy.test.ts` on clean `origin/main` as well

## Notes
- AI-assisted: yes
- This PR is intentionally scoped to the iMessage media handoff fix only.
- Verified the unrelated `monitor.media-policy` failure on a clean `origin/main` worktree before noting it here.

Closes #87597

commands.author: khang-dogo

target.title: fix(telegram): expose spooled handler timeout config
target.body:
## Summary
- expose `channels.telegram.spooledUpdateHandlerTimeoutMs` with top-level and per-account config support
- pass the configured value into Telegram isolated polling sessions
- document the timeout and regenerate bundled channel config metadata

## Why
Telegram isolated ingress already fails stale claimed spool updates after a handler timeout, but operators could not tune that timeout from `channels.telegram`. A wedged `.processing` file can therefore keep a same-chat lane blocked until the default 25 minute timeout.

## Runtime Proof
I ran a redacted local runtime harness against the PR head using the real `TelegramPollingSession`, real spool files, real reply-fence timeout recovery path, and a fake Telegram Bot runtime to avoid live network calls. The harness configured `isolatedIngress.spooledUpdateHandlerTimeoutMs = 200`, wrote two same-lane spooled updates, wedged update `42`, and verified update `43` drained only after timeout recovery restarted isolated ingress.

```json
{
  "configuredTimeoutMs": 200,
  "configuredAbortGraceMs": 200,
  "elapsedMs": 475,
  "workerCreateCount": 2,
  "botConstructCount": 2,
  "events": [
    "first:42",
    "second:43"
  ],
  "failedUpdateIds": [
    42
  ],
  "pendingUpdateIds": [],
  "timeoutLog": "[telegram] Telegram isolated polling spool handler timed out behind update 42 on lane telegram:-100:topic:10 after 205ms; marking the update failed, aborting active reply work, and restarting isolated ingress so later updates can drain."
}
```

This shows the configured timeout was honored by the isolated-ingress runtime path: update `42` was failed after the configured timeout budget and the later same-lane update `43` drained after the restart.

## Validation
- `pnpm config:channels:check`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/telegram/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/polling-session.test.ts` passed locally while preparing the patch. A later rerun in this workspace timed out in Vitest/Rolldown pre-test bundling with repeated plugin timing warnings before any assertion output.
